title: How QE changed banks, and why that makes QT dangerous
summary_raw: An interview with Jill Cetina

summary_ai.digest: Jill Cetina argues QE structurally altered bank balance sheets and funding models, making quantitative tightening riskier than policy makers assume because banks now depend on central bank liquidity support.
summary_ai.summary_long: The piece is an interview-based analysis of how a decade of QE reshaped bank assets, liabilities, and risk management—moving deposits and reserves to the center of the banking system and creating dependencies on central bank backstops. Cetina contends that QT (Fed balance sheet runoff) is unwinding that structure faster than banks and regulators anticipated, forcing rapid repricing of liquidity and funding costs. The implication is that the 2023 banking crisis was a harbinger, not a one-off, because structural fragility persists. For FIG investors and deal strategists, this frames regional-bank consolidation, deposit pricing power, and regulatory capital arbitrage as ongoing pressure points rather than cyclical headwinds.
talking_points: QE didn't just lower rates—it permanently reengineered bank balance sheets to rely on Fed reserves and wholesale funding, making QT a structural shock, not a policy dial turn.
The 2023 crisis exposed that banks hadn't fully repriced deposit funding and interest-rate risk; QT continues to pressure both, creating ongoing M&A and capital-raise triggers.
Regulatory and market discipline around bank liquidity buffers has tightened, but Cetina implies banks are still lagging the repricing—a signal for stress, consolidation, or regulatory action ahead.
summary_ai.highlight: QE didn't just lower rates—it rewired bank funding and risk models in ways QT is now unwinding faster than banks can adapt, keeping the sector vulnerable to shocks.

title: Incentives matter, installment #1637
summary_raw:
I had long wondered about this: Performance metrics can misalign individual and organizational incentives. We study a clean case: an NBA player holding the ball as a quarter expires must choose between a low-probability “heave” that can only help his team and protecting his shooting statistics. We model this decision as a metric-driven principal-agent problem […]

The post

summary_ai.digest: NBA players taking end-of-quarter heaves sacrifice team value to protect individual shooting percentages. A study models this as a principal-agent problem where performance metrics misalign player and team incentives.
summary_ai.summary_long: Researchers analyzed a clean incentive-misalignment case: NBA players at quarter-end must choose between low-probability "heave" shots that help their team win and shots that protect their individual shooting statistics. The study frames this as a principal-agent problem where the metric used to evaluate performance (shooting %) creates perverse incentives that conflict with organizational success (team winning). This is a textbook example of how metrics can be gamed when they don't fully capture the outcome that matters. The insight applies broadly to any organization where individual performance measurement diverges from collective goals.
talking_points: When you measure only what's easy to measure, rational actors optimize for the metric, not the mission — the NBA shooting % case is just one visible example.
Principal-agent problems aren't solved by better metrics alone; you need alignment between what the metric rewards and what the organization actually wants.
This frame explains why so many financial services roles (trading, origination, distribution) create perverse incentives that need constant policing.
summary_ai.highlight: A player protecting shooting statistics instead of taking a high-upside team shot at quarter-end is the same mechanism that drives mis-sold products and misaligned risk-taking in finance.

title: Betterment Faces Class Action Lawsuit Over Cash Sweep Accounts
summary_raw: A New Jersey client claims the robo advisor profited by sweeping excess cash into deposit accounts with participating banks that did not generate interest.

summary_ai.summary_long: A New Jersey plaintiff has filed a class action against Betterment, claiming the robo advisor systematically moved excess cash from client portfolios into sweep accounts at partner banks offering zero or near-zero interest rates. This echoes a 2015 SEC settlement with Betterment over similar practices, though the firm had since implemented interest-bearing sweep options. The lawsuit targets the firm's profitability model and fiduciary duty standards for digital advisors managing retail cash. The case surfaces competitive pressure in robo advisory as margins compress and AUM growth slows.
talking_points: Betterment is defending against allegations it used zero-interest sweep accounts to generate undisclosed revenue from client cash holdings—a practice it settled with the SEC over a decade ago.

The lawsuit raises fiduciary questions for the entire robo-advisory category: whether offering any sweep option (even low-rate) satisfies best-execution obligations when higher-yielding alternatives exist.
This creates regulatory risk for Betterment's business model and potential precedent for enforcement or advisory-rule tightening on cash management disclosure and sweep mechanics.
summary_ai.highlight: Class action alleges Betterment profited from sweeping client cash into non-interest-bearing accounts without disclosure—echoing a 2015 SEC settlement but raising renewed questions about robo-advisor fiduciary standards.

title: “Markets are competitive if and only if P != NP”
summary_raw:
I prove that competitive market outcomes require computational intractability. If P = NP, firms can efficiently solve the collusion detection problem, identifying deviations from cooperative agreements in complex, noisy markets and thereby making collusion sustainable as an equilibrium. If P != NP, the collusion detection problem is computationally infeasible for markets satisfying a natural instance-hardness […]

The post

summary_ai.digest: Tyler Cowen argues competitive markets require P ≠ NP: if P = NP, firms can efficiently detect collusion and sustain cartels; if P ≠ NP, collusion detection becomes computationally intractable, preserving competition as equilibrium.
summary_ai.summary_long: Cowen reframes market competition through computational complexity theory. The insight: collusion requires firms to detect when peers deviate from cooperative agreements in noisy, high-dimensional markets. If P = NP (all problems verifiable in polynomial time can be solved in polynomial time), this detection becomes tractable and collusion becomes rational and sustainable. Conversely, if P ≠ NP (the widely-held conjecture), collusion detection remains computationally infeasible, making competition a natural equilibrium because monitoring costs explode. This flips the usual antitrust framing: competition isn't enforced by law or ideology, but by the hardness of the computational problem itself. The piece bridges game theory, algorithmic complexity, and industrial organization.
talking_points: Collusion equilibrium depends on the computational tractability of deviation detection, not legal enforcement or firm intent.
If P = NP, firms can solve collusion-monitoring in polynomial time; if P ≠ NP (most likely), monitoring costs make competition inevitable regardless of cartel preferences.
This offers a novel lens on why certain markets remain competitive (high-dimensional pricing, many players) while others cartelize (transparent, low-dimensional agreements).
summary_ai.highlight: Competitive markets may be fundamentally sustained not by regulation but by computational intractability—a frame applicable to evaluating fintech platforms, trading venues, and lending markets where monitoring complexity varies.
